skill.name: docx
skill.description: Create, inspect, edit, and validate Microsoft Word .docx documents. The skill includes OOXML guidance, document helper scripts, tracked changes workflows, comments, formatting preservation, and text extraction.
skill.summary: Work with Word .docx files using OOXML, tracked changes, comments, and validation helpers.
skill.author: ArtemisAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, cryptography, and obfuscation patterns. Most high-volume hits are false positives from OOXML schemas, namespace URLs, examples, and documentation, with no prompt injection evidence found. The audit confirms a real high-risk issue: several helpers extract user-supplied Office archives with extractall() and no ZIP path validation.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Office Archive Extraction
high_findings.description: The skill extracts .docx and other Office files with zipfile extractall() without validating member paths. A crafted Office archive can use path traversal entries to write outside the intended output directory when processed.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct extractall() calls process user-controlled Office archives. I did not find path normalization or rejection of absolute or parent-directory ZIP members before extraction.

medium_findings.title: External Tool Execution Requires Trust Boundary
medium_findings.description: The skill runs local tools such as soffice and git through subprocess.run(). The command arrays are fixed, so command injection is unlikely, but document conversion tools parse attacker-controlled files and should run in a sandbox.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run() is confirmed, but arguments are hardcoded command arrays rather than shell strings. Residual risk comes from external parsers handling untrusted documents.

medium_findings.title: Privileged and Global Dependency Installation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends sudo apt-get and global npm installation for dependencies. This is not malicious by itself, but marketplace users should not execute elevated or global installs from a skill without environment approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The privileged and global install commands are explicit in the dependency instructions. They appear operational, not malicious, but they expand system-level impact.

low_findings.title: Static Keyword Findings in OOXML Schemas Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many cryptography, C2, network scanning, path traversal, and obfuscation hits are schema names, namespace URLs, XML imports, or long template namespace declarations. These are data definitions and examples, not executable malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are OOXML schema declarations, namespace values, enum names, or XML templates. They do not execute code or contact networks.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Standards Namespaces and Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings mostly identify OOXML namespace URIs and documentation examples. I did not find evidence that these URLs are fetched or used for data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear as namespace constants, relationship types, and hyperlink examples. No network client call was found around these cited lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe Office Archive Extraction
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill extracts .docx and other Office files with zipfile extractall() without validating member paths. A crafted Office archive can use path traversal entries to write outside the intended output directory when processed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct extractall() calls process user-controlled Office archives. I did not find path normalization or rejection of absolute or parent-directory ZIP members before extraction.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Tool Execution Requires Trust Boundary
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill runs local tools such as soffice and git through subprocess.run(). The command arrays are fixed, so command injection is unlikely, but document conversion tools parse attacker-controlled files and should run in a sandbox.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run() is confirmed, but arguments are hardcoded command arrays rather than shell strings. Residual risk comes from external parsers handling untrusted documents.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged and Global Dependency Installation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends sudo apt-get and global npm installation for dependencies. This is not malicious by itself, but marketplace users should not execute elevated or global installs from a skill without environment approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The privileged and global install commands are explicit in the dependency instructions. They appear operational, not malicious, but they expand system-level impact.

content.value_statement: Professional Word documents often require precise edits, comments, and tracked changes. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows for creating, unpacking, editing, validating, and repacking .docx files.
actual_capabilities: Creates new .docx files with the JavaScript docx library and documented formatting patterns.
Extracts document text to Markdown with Pandoc, including tracked change options.
Unpacks .docx files into OOXML parts for direct XML inspection and editing.
Adds comments, replies, tracked insertions, and tracked deletions through Python helper classes.
Repackages edited Office document folders into .docx, .pptx, or .xlsx archives.
Runs local validation checks for XML structure, relationships, content types, and redlining rules.
limitations: The helper scripts modify local files and should run only in a trusted workspace.
Untrusted Office archives require sandboxing because archive extraction lacks path validation.
Some workflows require external tools such as Pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, npm, and Python packages.
Complex formatting can still require manual OOXML review after automated edits.

use_cases.title: Prepare Contract Redlines
use_cases.description: Apply precise tracked insertions, deletions, and comments while preserving review history.

use_cases.title: Generate Client Deliverables
use_cases.description: Create structured Word reports with headings, tables, images, links, and consistent styles.

use_cases.title: Audit Document Structure
use_cases.description: Inspect OOXML parts, relationships, content types, comments, and validation issues in existing documents.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Document Text
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the text from this .docx file, preserve headings and tracked changes, and summarize the document structure.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Styled Document
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a .docx report with a title page, section headings, a table, bullet lists, and consistent Word styles.

prompt_templates.title: Add Review Comments
prompt_templates.prompt: Add comments to the attached .docx file on ambiguous language, missing definitions, and inconsistent dates.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Tracked Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Unpack this .docx, map the requested edits to OOXML, apply tracked changes in batches, validate them, and repack the document.

output_examples.input: Review a service agreement and mark suggested clause edits.
output: A revised Word document with tracked insertions and deletions.
Reviewer comments attached to specific clauses.
A short validation summary covering text changes and document integrity.

output_examples.input: Create a formatted project status report from notes.
output: A Word report with a clear title, sections, tables, and consistent typography.
A separate explanation of the document structure and formatting choices.

output_examples.input: Inspect a .docx file that may have broken relationships.
output: A list of missing references, invalid content type declarations, or XML validation errors.
Recommended repair steps before repacking the document.

best_practices: Run document scripts in an isolated workspace, especially when handling files from unknown sources.
Validate every repacked document before sending it to reviewers or clients.
Make tracked changes in small batches and verify each batch before continuing.
anti_patterns: Do not extract untrusted Office archives into sensitive directories.
Do not install global dependencies with elevated privileges without environment approval.
Do not edit OOXML text without checking how Word splits text across runs.

faq.question: Can this skill create new Word documents?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents a JavaScript docx workflow for generating .docx files with styles, tables, links, images, and headings.

faq.question: Can it edit existing .docx files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can unpack OOXML, edit XML parts with helper classes, validate the result, and repack the document.

faq.question: Does it support tracked changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes redlining guidance and Python helpers for tracked insertions, deletions, comment ranges, and validation.

faq.question: Does it require external software?
faq.answer: Some workflows require Pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, npm packages, and Python libraries such as defusedxml.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted documents?
faq.answer: Use caution. The archive extraction helpers need sandboxing because they do not validate extracted ZIP member paths.

faq.question: Can it preserve complex formatting?
faq.answer: It provides OOXML-level editing guidance, but complex layouts should be checked manually after automated changes.
